question: Prepare a speech on the title “The Will to Win” to be delivered before the class during a competition. Hints: Title Introduction Objective and Illustrations Specific examples Purpose of the title
answer:
The Will to Win
Introduction:
Respected Judges, teachers, and my dear friends, a very good morning to you all. Today, I stand before you to speak on a theme that has defined human history and shaped the destinies of legends: “The Will to Win.” What separates a dreamer from an achiever? It isn’t just talent, luck, or wealth. It is an unyielding, burning desire within the soul to conquer obstacles and reach the summit, no matter how steep the climb.
Objective and Illustrations:
The core objective of “the will to win” is not merely about collecting trophies or defeating others; it is about mastering oneself. It is the refusal to accept defeat when everything around you screams that you cannot succeed. Think of a tiny seed buried deep under heavy soil. It doesn’t have arms or eyes, yet it has an innate, silent will to fight the darkness, pierce through the hard earth, and ultimately win its place under the sun as a mighty tree. That is the essence of this will it is life’s natural urge to triumph.
Specific Examples:
History proves that grit triumphs over circumstance. Consider Dr. APJ Abdul Kalam, who rose from a humble boatman’s family in Rameswaram to become the Missile Man and President of India. Look at Wilma Rudolph, who was told she would never walk again due to polio, yet her fierce will to win made her an Olympic champion with three gold medals. They showed us that limitations exist only in the mind.
Purpose of the Title & Sources for Implementation:
The purpose of this title is to remind us that winning is a choice, not a coincidence. So, where do we find the resources to build this mindset? We do not need to buy it from outside. The primary source lies within our own minds it is called discipline. Other resources include the inspiring biographies of great leaders, the guidance of our teachers and parents, and our own daily habits. When we embrace failure as a lesson rather than a dead end, we actively fuel our inner will.
Usefulness and Benefits:
Developing this attitude is incredibly useful, especially for us as students. We often face academic pressure, stress, and anxiety about the future. A strong will to win builds resilience. It teaches us to stay calm during exams, converts our self-doubt into self-confidence, and ensures that we do not quit when a math problem gets too tough or a concept seems impossible to grasp. It transforms us from passive spectators of life into active achievers.
Conclusion:
In conclusion, my dear friends, remember that the will to win is pointless without the will to prepare. Success demands sweat, sacrifice, and sleepless nights. As we step out into the competitive world, let us promise ourselves never to back down. To quote the famous words of Vince Lombardi: “Winning is not a sometime thing; it’s an all-the-time thing.” So, ignite that fire within you, believe in your potential, and conquer your dreams!
Thank you, and Jai Hind.
subject: English
chapter: English Kumarbharati [English] Standard 10  chapter 2.3 - Connecting the Dots

question: Collect more information about Steve Jobs with the help of the Internet. Complete it with images into a file.
answer:
Jobs was raised by adoptive parents in Cupertino, California, located in what is now known as Silicon Valley. Though he was interested in engineering, his passions of youth varied. He dropped out of Reed College in Portland, Oregon, took a job at Atari Corporation as a video game designer in early 1974, and saved enough money for a pilgrimage to India to experience Buddhism.
Back in Silicon Valley in the autumn of 1974, Jobs reconnected with Stephen Wozniak, a former high school friend who worked for Hewlett-Packard. When Wozniak told Jobs of his progress in designing his own computer logic board, Jobs suggested that they go into business together, which they did after Hewlett-Packard formally turned down Wozniak’s design in 1976. The Apple I, as they called the logic board, was built in the Jobses’ family garage with money they obtained by selling Jobs’ Volkswagen minibus and Wozniak’s programmable calculator.
subject: English
chapter: English Kumarbharati [English] Standard 10  chapter 2.3 - Connecting the Dots

question: Do you remember doing some activities in your childhood that you didn’t like it. Form pairs and make a list of all those activities.
answer:
Our Pair’s List of Disliked Childhood Activities
My partner and I compared notes and realised we shared many of the same struggles growing up. Here is our list of activities we absolutely hated doing as kids:

Waking up early on cold winter mornings for school.
Practicing cursive handwriting sheets over and over.
Doing daily mathematics drills and memorising multiplication tables.
Packing the school bag according to the timetable every single night.

Sharing the Experience with the Class
Good morning everyone,
Looking at our list, the one activity that I absolutely detested as a child was packing my school bag every single night according to the timetable. It felt like a boring, repetitive chore, and I often complained to my parents about why I couldn’t just keep all my books in my bag all the time.
But looking back, I realise that this specific activity has immensely helped me solve problems today.
That simple, annoying habit of packing my bag taught me organisation and foresight. Today, we face a massive syllabus, project deadlines, and exam schedules. Because I was forced to look at my timetable every night as a kid, I naturally learned how to plan my day ahead. It stopped me from being disorganised. When I face a heavy study load now, I don’t panic; I break it down, organise my materials, and plan my schedule just like I used to plan my school bag.
It taught me that solving big problems in life often requires daily discipline and preparation the night before. It turns out that boring childhood chores were actually training for my future!
subject: English
chapter: English Kumarbharati [English] Standard 10  chapter 2.3 - Connecting the Dots

question: The poet has used the word REST thrice in the poem. Write what the word implies in each of the three lines it occurs.
answer:
The meaning of the word ‘Rest’ in the 10th line is twofold: it means ‘remaining’, and it also means the gift of ‘rest’. The line is an example of Pun in which the poet wishes to say that the remaining gifts lay at the bottom of the glass along with the gift of ‘rest’.
In the 14th line, the word ‘rest’ means to ‘credit’. The line conveys the idea that if God were to bestow ‘rest’ on man, man would extend this credit to nature rather than to God.
The word ‘rest’ in the 16th line of the poem means ‘the remaining’. Here, the poet, on behalf of God, asks man to keep all the remaining gifts except the gift of ‘rest’.
subject: English
chapter: English Kumarbharati [English] Standard 10  chapter 2.4 - The Pulley

question: What does God want in return from man, for the gifts He has bestowed upon him?
answer: In return for the gift He has bestowed on man, God wants him to lead a life of goodness and to keep on adoring the God who has been so generous to him.
subject: English

question: Why did God withhold the gift of ‘Rest’ from man?
answer: ‘Rest’ makes us feel satisfied and contented. When we have ‘rest’, we desire nothing more. We might even stop wanting to know more about God, our Creator, or seeking His blessings. Hence, God withheld the gift of ‘Rest’ from man.
subject: English

question: Besides those given in the poem, what other gifts has God blessed mankind with above his other creations? Enlist them.
answer: Besides those mentioned in the extract, God has blessed mankind with an abundance of natural creations, such as trees, birds and animals, fruits, and flowers. He has bestowed on mankind the power of speech, the ability to read and write, and the use of language. Above all, He has given human beings a marvellous brain, with which man has invented millions of things, right from the wheel to the computer.

question: Discuss with your group and justify the title of the poem ‘The Pulley’ in your own words.
answer: The pulley works only when it is pulled by someone’s hands. The poet has given the poem the title ‘The Pulley’ to express the idea that only when ‘repining restlessness’ keeps man discontented despite his abundance of riches and graces, will he be drawn towards God. Just as a pulley draws something from a lower position to a higher position. In the same way, man’s thoughts will be lifted towards God, it’s the creator.

question: Write Critical Appreciation of the poem in a paragraph format. Points: Title Poet Rhyme scheme Favourite line Theme/Central idea Figures of speech
answer:
The Pulley
The title of the poem is ‘The Pulley’. The poet is George Herbert.
The central idea of the poem is the reason for man’s continual restlessness throughout his life. According to the poet, this is because God withheld the quality of rest from man, so that man’s thoughts would ultimately turn towards God, his creator
Each verse of the poem has five lines. The rhyme scheme of each verse is a-b-a-b-a.
The chief figure of speech used in the poem is Metaphor. The glass of blessings signifies the sum of all human qualities bestowed on man. The quality of ‘rest’ or ‘contentment’ is implicitly compared to a jewel. The other figures of speech are Pun, Inversion, Paradox, etc.
This is a metaphysical poem with a message. And the message is that, however rich we may be, or whatever the abundance of qualities we may have, we must always turn our thoughts to God and thank Him for the many blessings He has bestowed on us. The poem is rich in imagery and monologues. It is an example of an extended metaphor.
My favourite line in this poem is ‘Let him be rich and weary’. This brings out the paradox of man’s nature so well. Even the richest man in the world eventually grows tired and becomes discontented. This is what God intended for man.
I like this poem very much because it teaches me not to be self-satisfied and contented but to always remember and thank God for all the blessings He has bestowed on me.
subject: English
chapter: English Kumarbharati [English] Standard 10  chapter 2.4 - The Pulley

question: ‘Pun’ can be defined as a play on words based on their different meanings. Example: ‘Writing with a broken pencil is pointless.’ In this poem, there is an example of Pun.

question: Write a paragraph on the points in each block given below to get a summary of each of the four stanzas of the poem.
answer: God created man as one of his best creations. While creating him, he kept the glass of blessings close. He desired to pour out to him all the gifts he possibly had. He contracted all the world’s wealth, which lay dispersed on the face of the earth, and blessed man with it.
subject: English
chapter: English Kumarbharati [English] Standard 10  chapter 2.4 - The Pulley

question: Write a paragraph on the points in the block given below to get a summary of the four stanzas of the poem. God gifted man Beauty, wisdom, honour,etc. Did not give ‘Rest’
answer: God blessed his creation with gifts such as beauty, wisdom, and honour. He knew men would use them to succeed and prosper. But he was also aware of the flaw in his creation: his own conceitedness. Hence, although he gave man all the gifts and blessings, what God deliberately kept away from him was the gift of rest, i.e. satisfaction.
subject: English
chapter: English Kumarbharati [English] Standard 10  chapter 2.4 - The Pulley

question: Write a paragraph on the points in the block given below to get a summary of the four stanzas of the poem. Man If happy/contented Forgets God Instead worships Gifts
answer: God has blessed man with ample gifts and blessings. Man, too, made use of his blessings in his search for happiness and progress. But the weakness within man is that he· tends to forget his Creator once he achieves success. He instead begins to value and worship the. Gifts rather than his Creator.
subject: English
chapter: English Kumarbharati [English] Standard 10  chapter 2.4 - The Pulley

question: Write a paragraph on the points in the block given below to get a summary of the four stanzas of the poem. Man needs to be reminded of the Giver God made his life discontented
answer: In his search for happiness and progress, man forgets the one who bestowed blessings on him. Thus, the conceited creature that he is, he must be reminded to thank his Creator. With this purpose, God deprives man of satisfaction and rest at the end of his success. Weary from achieving all the success, man craves satisfaction and peace and, as a result, has to go back to his Creator for them.
subject: English
chapter: English Kumarbharati [English] Standard 10  chapter 2.4 - The Pulley

question: Think and give your own response. Name any other social activist who has worked/is working earnestly for child-welfare. Write about his/her activities.
answer: Kirti Bharti from Rajasthan, a 29-year-old child welfare activist, is a saviour to children whose parents force them into marrying at a young age. So far, she has prevented 900 such marriages and helped annul (end) 150 such underage alliances. Of late, on behalf of the Saarthi Trust, the organisation she began in 2012, for doing this noble work, she has been receiving death threats from villagers, caste councils and local politicians. People in her state still resort to honour killing, which leaves children traumatised. Bharti is an angel of hope for these children and a strong inspiration for the youth.
subject: English

We must not think What can one person do? Every little drop counts. If each of us makes a little bit of effort, the sum total will be substantial.

This can be done only by appealing to their hearts and emotions, which the speaker has attempted to do.

question: What will you do in the following situation? If you see a child working in a restaurant
answer: I will talk to the restaurant owner and ask him to send the child to a government school. I will offer to give the child my old books and clothes.
subject: English
chapter: English Kumarbharati [English] Standard 10  chapter 2.5 - Let’s March

question: What will you do in the following situation? If you find a child working at a construction site.
answer: I will talk to the parents and ask them to send him to school. I will also inform my parents and ask them to help.
subject: English
chapter: English Kumarbharati [English] Standard 10  chapter 2.5 - Let’s March

question: What will you do in the following situation? If you find a child working at a brickwork site.
answer: I will find an NGO and inform them about it.
subject: English
chapter: English Kumarbharati [English] Standard 10  chapter 2.5 - Let’s March

question: What will you do in the following situation? If you come across a beggar child.
answer: I will find an orphanage or NGO in the area and ask them to help him. I will also give him some picture books so that he begins to take an interest in books.
subject: English
chapter: English Kumarbharati [English] Standard 10  chapter 2.5 - Let’s March

question: Write any 2 efforts that you can make to enrol deprived children/out of school children into a school. One is given for you. I will persuade parents of such children to send them to school.
answer:
I will persuade parents of such children to send them to school.
I would teach them to read, write, and count myself.
I will approach an NGO to get them help, so that they can go to school.
I will contact a local school and speak to the authorities to enrol the children there.
I would meet and encourage the rich families in my area to finance the education of at least one poor child.
subject: English
chapter: English Kumarbharati [English] Standard 10  chapter 2.5 - Let’s March
